content:
Hello again! I'm working on this GitHub issue as my first contribution to the kernel.

I read through the rust for linux contributing docs, as well as scanned through the kernel patch docs.

My understanding of the process I should follow is:

  • Check out the "rust-next" branch of https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux
  • Make code changes
  • Regenerate the docs using make LLVM=1 rustdoc
  • Render the docs and look at them - verifying that the ones I altered still look as expected
  • Compile the code with Clippy enabled using make LLVM=1 CLIPPY=1
  • Format the code by running make LLVM=1 rustfmt
  • Verify Rust tests pass with make LLVM=1 rusttest
  • Verify KUnit tests pass with ./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --make_options LLVM=1 --arch x86_64 --kconfig_add CONFIG_RUST=y
  • Commit the code, also signing off the commit with git commit -s
  • Check patch with scripts/checkpatch.pl - in particular using the --codespell option
  • Use these commands to generate maintainers, reviewers, and mailing lists
awk '/^RUST$/,/^$/' MAINTAINERS | grep '^M:'    | cut -f2- | xargs -IP echo --to \'P\' \\
awk '/^RUST$/,/^$/' MAINTAINERS | grep '^[RL]:' | cut -f2- | xargs -IP echo --cc \'P\' \\
  • Make sure to follow the canonical patch format
  • Add Suggested-by: tag and Link: tag pointing to https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/1064
  • Submit patch using git send-email

Does this sound correct? Is there anything missing? (This is my first time using a patch based contribution workflow and I want to be sure to get it right)

Sounds about right. I would say git send-email could be a challenging part since everybody has their own email setup. Using b4 may be helpful in some cases, but I also heard people saying b4 doesn't work very well for them.

One thing about git send-email is that it has a --dry-run option, you can always start with that to be safe.
Nice! I didn't know about that option. Ty!
How (not) to send emails to Linus Torvalds incidentally
that is exactly what I'm scared of!

Took a deeper look at this today.

I've found 3 instances where use kernel::prelude::* is used in doctests.

  • rust/kernel/workqueue.rs
  • rust/kernel/net/phy.rs
  • rust/macros/lib.rs

The first of these, in rust/kernel/workqueue.rs, was pretty self explanatory. I removed it, re-rendered the docs, then ran the kunit tests and the tests passed. I previewed the doc in html and it renders fine, just with the use kernel::prelude::* statement removed.

I then tried removing the second one, in rust/kernel/net/phy.rs. The kunit tests pass fine, but I cannot for the life of me figure out where in the rendered docs this one is.

Looking at Documentation/output/rust/rustdoc/kernel/net/index.html, I see this:

image.png

The third one, at rust/macros/lib.rs, is interesting. It has an ignore attribute, which I believe means it is not tested. I'm unsure whether this one should be removed.

My questions are:
1) How do I check the render for the rustdoc in rust/kernel/net/phy.rs?
2) Should I remove the use kernel::prelude::* line from rust/macros/lib.rs?
Do you have CONFIG_RUST_PHYLIB_ABSTRACTIONS enabled? The phy module in net is not compiled if it's not set
Aha! That must be it
thank you!

The examples in macros/lib.rs are ignored because the module macro declares some globals that don't compose well and cause build breaks. Since then, we have found a workaround: declare the examples under a module, see an example here: https://github.com/wedsonaf/linux/blob/ext2fs/rust/kernel/fs.rs#L439 -- perhaps you want to do the same here to remove the ignore tag?
I will check that out

Nell Shamrell-Harrington said:

Took a deeper look at this today.

I've found 3 instances where use kernel::prelude::* is used in doctests.

  • rust/kernel/workqueue.rs
  • rust/kernel/net/phy.rs
  • rust/macros/lib.rs

Note that it might not always be imported via kernel::prerlude::*, it could also be done via eg kernel::{prelude::*, ...}

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

The examples in macros/lib.rs are ignored because the module macro declares some globals that don't compose well and cause build breaks. Since then, we have found a workaround: declare the examples under a module, see an example here: https://github.com/wedsonaf/linux/blob/ext2fs/rust/kernel/fs.rs#L439 -- perhaps you want to do the same here to remove the ignore tag?

Would this be something that should be done as two separate patches?
Yes. They can be in the same series, but in separate patches

I seem to have somehow gotten myself in a bad state:

azureuser@nell-linux-vm-03-21:~/linux$ make LLVM=1 -j17
  SYNC    include/config/auto.conf.cmd
scripts/.generate_rust_target.cmd:1: *** missing separator.  Stop.
any advice on how to fix this? (deleting and recloning the repo does work, but I'm wondering if there is a better way)
did you try make LLVM=1 clean?
this seems to have happened in between me finishing work yesterday, turning off the VM, then turning it back on this morning
oooh....
trying that
that did the trick! ty!
no problem :)

Since then, we have found a workaround: declare the examples under a module, see an example here: https://github.com/wedsonaf/linux/blob/ext2fs/rust/kernel/fs.rs#L439 -- perhaps you want to do the same here to remove the ignore tag?

I haven't been able to make this work, unfortunately. However, I did get everything else working (including another example where I was able to remove the ignore tag). Would anyone object to me submitting a patch with what I have working?
I've been tracking what files I have investigated/changed here https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/1064#issuecomment-2030345497

Since then, we have found a workaround: declare the examples under a module, see an example here: https://github.com/wedsonaf/linux/blob/ext2fs/rust/kernel/fs.rs#L439 -- perhaps you want to do the same here to remove the ignore tag?

I haven't been able to make this work, unfortunately. However, I did get everything else working (including another example where I was able to remove the ignore tag). Would anyone object to me submitting a patch with what I have working?

what error are you getting?

when running the rust tests:

---- rust/macros/lib.rs - module (line 29) stdout ----
error: cannot find macro `pr_info` in this scope
  --> rust/macros/lib.rs:63:9
   |
36 |         pr_info!("i32 param is:  {}\n", my_i32.read());
   |         ^^^^^^^

error: cannot find macro `pr_info` in this scope
  --> rust/macros/lib.rs:59:13
   |
32 |             pr_info!("i32 param is:  {}\n", writeable_i32.read(&lock));
   |             ^^^^^^^

 --> rust/macros/lib.rs:31:1
  |

  --> rust/macros/lib.rs:58:24

For more information about an error, try `rustc --explain E0425`.
Couldn't compile the test.

failures:
    rust/macros/lib.rs - module (line 29)

test result: FAILED. 1 passed; 1 failed; 9 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out; finished in 0.08s`
when removing the ignore tag on this example https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/768409cff6cc89fe1194da880537a09857b6e4db/rust/macros/lib.rs#L30
did you try wrapping it in a mod?
(you might also need to add some imports in the module)
I did - and saw the same issue - but I think adding the imports to the module might do the trick. Trying that now

Just to verify - is this the correct way to wrap this example in a mod?

/// # mod module_kernel_module_sample {
/// module!{
///     type: MyModule,
///     name: "my_kernel_module",
///     author: "Rust for Linux Contributors",
///     description: "My very own kernel module!",
///     license: "GPL",
///     params: {
///        my_i32: i32 {
///            default: 42,
///            permissions: 0o000,
///            description: "Example of i32",
///        },
///        writeable_i32: i32 {
///            default: 42,
///            permissions: 0o644,
///            description: "Example of i32",
///        },
///    },
/// }
///
/// struct MyModule;
///
/// impl kernel::Module for MyModule {
///     fn init() -> Result<Self> {
///         // If the parameter is writeable, then the kparam lock must be
///         // taken to read the parameter:
///         {
///             let lock = THIS_MODULE.kernel_param_lock();
///             pr_info!("i32 param is:  {}\n", writeable_i32.read(&lock));
///         }
///         // If the parameter is read only, it can be read without locking
///         // the kernel parameters:
///         pr_info!("i32 param is:  {}\n", my_i32.read());
///         Ok(Self)
///     }
/// }
/// # }
/// ```

yes that looks correct.

did adding the imports not work?
I'm still figuring out how to import things from the kernel crate into this example
I'm used to doing that through a Cargo.toml, but those don't seem to be used in this area
adding use kernel::prelude::*; inside the mod should import most of them

This is the error I get when adding that (back) in

error[E0433]: failed to resolve: use of undeclared crate or module `kernel`
 --> rust/macros/lib.rs:31:5
  |
4 | use kernel::prelude::*;
  |     ^^^^^^ use of undeclared crate or module `kernel`

Nell Shamrell-Harrington said:

I'm used to doing that through a Cargo.toml, but those don't seem to be used in this area

ah, we don't have a Cargo.toml. Also all dependencies should be from the kernel crate.
that makes sense
I seem to be having trouble resolving dependencies from the kernel crate in this doc example

Nell Shamrell-Harrington said:

This is the error I get when adding that (back) in

error[E0433]: failed to resolve: use of undeclared crate or module `kernel`
 --> rust/macros/lib.rs:31:5
  |
4 | use kernel::prelude::*;
  |     ^^^^^^ use of undeclared crate or module `kernel`

Ah I remember, the macros crate is a build requirement of kernel, so you can't import it there.
I don't think we're going to be able to remove the ignore tag from this example
yeah I think so too
but it was certainly worth a try :)
Yeah, sorry that you had to waste time on that
no worries - it helped me get to know the code base a little better
and, incidentally, compiling the kernel on a 16 core system provides the perfect break to fold some laundry :)
yeah it does take some time
going to prepare a patch with what I have (and will likely submit it tomorrow morning)
thank you for the help, @Benno Lossin !
no problem!

Finally got the patch series ready.

I send the patch series to myself first to check formatting, etc. Would anyone mind reviewing the format of these emails before I send them to the appropriate maintainers, reviewers, and email lists?

And I'm assuming that when I am ready to send the patches for real (!) I should use a command along these lines (ensuring that all the appropriate maintainers/reviewers/lists receive it).

git send-email \
--to=ojeda@kernel.org \
--to=alex.gaynor@gmail.com \
--to=wedsonaf@gmail.com \
--cc=boqun.feng@gmail.com \
--cc=gary@garyguo.net \
--cc=bjorn3_gh@protonmail.com \
--cc=benno.lossin@proton.me \
--cc=a.hindborg@samsung.com \
--cc=aliceryhl@google.com \
--cc=rust-for-linux@vger.kernel.org \
0001-rust-remove-unneeded-kernel-prelude-imports-from-doc.patch \
0002-rust-remove-ignore-tag-kernel-prelude-from-doc.patch

Looks good as far as I can tell. Only two minor things:

  • The splitting into commits looks a bit strange, why not include the prelude removal in the second patch in the first?
  • With the space you gain in the commit title of the second commit, could you add why the change is necessary?

Did you run scripts/checkpatch.pl <patch-files> (it checks for some basic formatting of a patch)? Also if you're not sure if you got the right people, you can run scripts/get_maintainer.pl <patch-files>.

For the commit messages, I have these nits:

  • I would leave out the "This ..." so instead of "This removes explicit kernel::prelude" I would write "Removes explicit kernel::prelude"
  • I would put code into backtics

Otherwise looks good!

On top of what Valentin and Benno suggested, I would also suggest taking a look at --codespell and --strict for checkpatch.pl. For git-send-email, please also add --cc linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org. Thanks for working on this Nell!
Thank you for the feedback! I am traveling this week, but will endeavor to get that all incorporated within the next day or two, then submit the patch

Made the edits (I decided to remove the second patch to keep this one as clean and focused as possible - I'm open to revisiting removing ignore from the rustdocs where possible in the future), ran all the checks, now I'm submitting the patch...
sent!
